findings.title: Silent no-op when handler is not registered for scheduled job
findings.description: When scheduleJob() is called but no handler is registered for the record's handler name, the method silently returns. The job record is already persisted to the database (save() is called before scheduleJob() in at()/every()/cron()), so the job exists in the DB but will never fire. On start(), these jobs will also be silently skipped. The caller receives a valid job ID, creating the illusion of success. There is no warning, no error, and no way to detect this silent failure. On restart via start(), the orphaned job will again silently fail to schedule.

findings.title: Handler errors propagate unhandled from async cron callback
findings.description: The try/finally block has no catch clause. If handler() throws, the error propagates out of the async callback. Croner may or may not handle unhandled rejections from its callbacks -- this leads to unhandled promise rejections in Node.js, which can crash the process. Additionally, the finally block increments runCount even on failure, misrepresenting the job execution state (it looks like the job ran successfully when it actually threw).

findings.title: Package has no consumer in the monorepo
findings.code_snippet:
export { CronService } from './cron-service'
export type { CronJobRecord, JobHandler, ScheduleMode, ScheduleOptions } from './cron-types'
export { JobStore } from './job-store'
findings.description: No other package, app, or service in the monorepo imports @proj-airi/cron-service. The package is entirely orphaned -- it is built and tested, but not wired to any entry point (no Electron service, no IPC handler, no event listener). Per integration rules, code without a live entry point is dead-on-arrival. This change delivers a complete package in isolation, but no vertical slice is exercised. There is no entry point in any app that uses CronService to schedule a real job.
findings.suggestion: Wire CronService into the Electron main process service layer (apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/) or another app that needs scheduled job execution. Register it with injeca DI and connect it to at least one real consumer. Add at least a minimal integration: register CronService in the Electron main process, wire it to one concrete use case (e.g., periodic persona mood decay, context cleanup, or proactive trigger evaluation), and prove the end-to-end path works with an integration test.

findings.title: No logging in cron-service package
findings.code_snippet: // No import of @guiiai/logg anywhere in the package
findings.description: The entire cron-service package has zero logging. Critical operations -- job scheduling, job firing, handler failures, job removal, service start/stop -- all happen silently. In production, debugging scheduled job issues will be impossible without structured logs.
findings.suggestion: Add @guiiai/logg as a dependency and inject a logger using the useLogg('cron-service') pattern. Log at minimum: (1) INFO on job scheduled/removed/started/stopped, (2) ERROR on handler failure with job context, (3) WARN on missing handler registration, (4) DEBUG on each job tick/fire.

findings.title: No validation on schedule parameters (cron expr, interval, date)
findings.description: None of the three scheduling methods validate their inputs: (1) at() accepts any string without validating it as an ISO date or valid Date object. (2) every() accepts any number including 0, negative numbers, NaN, and Infinity. An intervalSeconds of 0 could cause a tight infinite loop. (3) cron() passes arbitrary strings to Croner without pre-validation, deferring errors to the library at scheduling time rather than at the API boundary. (4) ScheduleOptions.handler is not validated for empty strings.
findings.suggestion: Add input validation at the API boundary: (1) Validate date is a valid Date or parseable ISO string that is in the future. (2) Validate intervalSeconds is a positive finite integer (> 0, Number.isFinite, Number.isInteger). (3) Validate cron expression format or wrap Croner construction in try/catch with a clear error. (4) Validate handler name is non-empty. Use Valibot for schema validation per project conventions.

findings.title: Race condition in runCount increment for overlapping job executions
findings.description: The callback reads the current runCount from the database, then later writes runCount + 1. If handler execution is async and takes longer than the interval period (e.g., a 1-second interval with a 3-second handler), the next invocation reads the same runCount before the first writes its update. Result: lost increments. Additionally, there is no concurrency guard to prevent overlapping executions of the same job.
findings.suggestion: Use an atomic SQL UPDATE: 'UPDATE cron_jobs SET last_run_at = ?, run_count = run_count + 1 WHERE id = ?' instead of reading then writing. Also add a concurrency guard (e.g., a running Set<string>) to prevent overlapping executions of the same job, or configure Croner's protect option.

findings.title: Handler errors in cron callback propagate unhandled
findings.description: The try/finally block has no catch. If handler(currentRecord) throws, the error propagates into croner's internal scheduler. Depending on croner's behavior, this may silently kill the cron job or crash the process. There is no logging, no error context, and no way for the caller to know a job failed. Additionally, the finally block increments runCount even on failure, recording a 'successful' run for a failed execution.

findings.title: updateLastRun in finally block can mask original error
findings.description: The finally block calls this.store.updateLastRun(), which is a database operation that can throw (e.g., DB closed, disk full, concurrent write error). If the handler also threw, the DB error from finally will mask the original handler error, making debugging extremely difficult. The original error is lost entirely.

findings.title: Cron constructor can throw, leaving orphaned DB records
findings.code_snippet:
switch (record.mode) {
  case 'at':
    cronJob = new Cron(new Date(record.schedule), cronOptions, callback)
    break
  case 'every':
    cronJob = new Cron('* * * * * *', { ...cronOptions, interval: Number(record.schedule) }, callback)
    break
  case 'cron':
    cronJob = new Cron(record.schedule, cronOptions, callback)
    break
}
findings.description: The Cron constructor throws for invalid cron expressions, invalid dates, or invalid timezone values. Since at(), every(), and cron() call store.save() before scheduleJob(), a failure here leaves an orphaned record in the database that can never be scheduled. The error propagates as a raw croner library error with no context about which job failed or why.

findings.title: start() loop aborts on first scheduleJob failure
findings.description: If scheduleJob throws on any single job (e.g., invalid cron expression in stored record), the entire start() loop aborts, leaving all remaining jobs unscheduled. This is a partial-failure scenario where one bad record prevents the entire service from starting. There is no per-job error isolation.

findings.title: pause() and resume() public methods have zero test coverage
findings.description: CronService exposes pause() and resume() as public API methods (exported via index.ts), but neither has any test coverage. These methods modify runtime scheduling state and their behavior for both valid and invalid job IDs is completely unverified.
findings.suggestion: Add tests verifying: (1) pause() stops a recurring job from firing, (2) resume() restarts a paused job, (3) pause()/resume() on non-existent jobId returns false, (4) pause then resume restores original behavior.

findings.title: Handler error propagation in scheduleJob callback untested
findings.description: The scheduleJob callback wraps handler execution in try/finally to ensure updateLastRun is called even when the handler throws. This is a critical resilience behavior -- a failing handler should not prevent run tracking from updating. However, no test verifies this behavior.
findings.suggestion: Add a test that registers a handler which throws an Error, triggers it via at() with a short delay, waits for execution, then verifies the job's runCount was incremented and lastRunAt was updated despite the error.

findings.title: start() filtering logic (expired/disabled jobs) has no test
findings.description: start() contains two important filtering conditions: (1) skip disabled jobs, and (2) skip expired 'at' jobs. Neither filter branch is tested. If these conditions regress, disabled jobs would fire on restart and expired one-time jobs would attempt to re-execute.
findings.suggestion: Add two tests: (1) 'start() does not schedule disabled jobs' -- save a job with enabled=false, call start(), verify it does not fire. (2) 'start() skips expired at jobs' -- save an at-mode job with a past date, call start(), verify it is not re-scheduled.

findings.title: CronJobRecord schedule is polymorphic string -- illegal states representable
findings.description: CronJobRecord uses a single `schedule: string` whose semantics change based on `mode`: ISO date for 'at', numeric seconds for 'every', cron expression for 'cron'. This means the type permits nonsensical combinations (e.g., mode='at' with schedule='*/5 * * * *'). A discriminated union would make these illegal states unrepresentable.

findings.title: CronService.get() and list() leak mutable CronJobRecord references
findings.description: get() and list() return mutable CronJobRecord objects. Consumers can mutate fields (e.g., record.enabled = false, record.runCount = 999) creating inconsistency between the in-memory object and the persisted DB state.
findings.suggestion: Add `readonly` modifier to all CronJobRecord fields, or return a frozen copy: `return Object.freeze({...record})`. Prefer readonly interface approach.

findings.title: CronService hardcodes JobStore dependency via direct instantiation
findings.description: CronService directly instantiates JobStore in its constructor, violating the Dependency Inversion Principle. The service depends on a concrete class rather than an abstraction. This makes it impossible to swap the persistence layer without modifying CronService. The project uses injeca for DI, but this package does not participate in it.
findings.suggestion: Define a JobStore interface (e.g., ICronJobStore) in cron-types.ts with the save/get/list/remove/updateLastRun/close methods. Accept it via constructor injection: constructor(store: ICronJobStore).

findings.title: Unsafe 'as' cast on mode field from database row
findings.code_snippet: mode: row.mode as CronJobRecord['mode'],
findings.description: The mode field from the database row is cast to ScheduleMode ('at' | 'every' | 'cron') without runtime validation. While the CHECK constraint in the schema should prevent invalid values, data corruption or manual DB edits could produce unexpected values.
findings.suggestion: Add a runtime assertion or validation: validate that row.mode is one of the allowed values before the cast.

findings.title: close() does not use try/finally for resource cleanup
findings.description: If this.stop() throws, this.store.close() is never called, leaking the SQLite database connection.

findings.title: No use-after-close guard on JobStore
findings.description: After close() is called, any subsequent call to save(), get(), list(), remove(), or updateLastRun() will throw a raw better-sqlite3 error. There is no guard or meaningful error message.

findings.title: No Valibot validation at JobStore boundary -- unsafe `as` cast in mapRow
findings.description: mapRow() casts `row.mode` (a string from SQLite) to `ScheduleMode` using `as` without runtime validation. Valibot is the project standard for validation but is not used here.
findings.suggestion: Add a runtime guard or Valibot schema for the full row validation.

findings.title: Timestamps as bare `string`, intervalSeconds lacks positive constraint
findings.code_snippet:
createdAt: string
  lastRunAt: string | null
  runCount: number
findings.description: `createdAt` and `lastRunAt` are ISO date strings with no type-level distinction from arbitrary strings. `intervalSeconds` parameter in CronService.every() is `number` with no validation that it is positive.
findings.suggestion: Consider branded types for timestamps. For intervalSeconds, add a runtime check: `if (intervalSeconds <= 0) throw new Error('intervalSeconds must be positive')`.

findings.title: ScheduleOptions.handler is untyped string -- no link to registered handlers
findings.description: The `handler` field is a plain `string` that must match a key registered via `CronService.registerHandler()`. Typos in handler names are only caught at runtime (and silently).
findings.suggestion: Use a generic parameter on CronService to constrain handler names, or extract a HandlerName branded type.

findings.title: CronJobRecord mixes configuration and runtime state in single interface
findings.code_snippet:
export interface CronJobRecord {
  id: string
  name: string
  ...
  lastRunAt: string | null
  runCount: number
}
findings.description: CronJobRecord conflates immutable configuration (id, name, mode, schedule, handler, payload, timezone, createdAt) with mutable runtime state (enabled, lastRunAt, runCount). No separate creation input vs stored record type.
findings.suggestion: Split into CronJobConfig (immutable), CronJobState (mutable runtime), and CronJobRecord = CronJobConfig & CronJobState.

findings.title: stop() method behavior not directly tested
findings.description: stop() is called indirectly via close() in afterEach cleanup, but no test verifies its behavioral contract: that all actively running jobs cease to fire after stop() is called.
findings.suggestion: Add a test: schedule a recurring job, verify it fires at least once, call stop(), wait, and verify the count did not increase.

findings.title: No test for scheduling without registered handler behavior
findings.description: The existing test verifies the job is persisted without a handler, but does not assert the behavioral consequence: that the job is NOT scheduled. The startup ordering edge case (start before registerHandler) is also untested.
findings.suggestion: Add assertions verifying unregistered handler behavior and startup ordering scenarios.

findings.title: No boundary tests for invalid inputs (bad cron, negative interval)
findings.description: No tests verify behavior with invalid inputs: invalid cron expression, negative or zero interval, past date, empty handler name.
findings.suggestion: Add boundary tests for each invalid input scenario to document the API contract for edge cases.

findings.title: Exported CronService class has no JSDoc on any public method
findings.description: CronService is the primary public API of the package with 12 public methods and zero JSDoc comments. Key behaviors are non-obvious: at() accepts Date|string, every() takes seconds not milliseconds, start() rehydrates persisted jobs.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to CronService class and each public method documenting key semantics.

findings.title: Exported interfaces CronJobRecord and ScheduleOptions lack JSDoc
findings.description: All exported types lack JSDoc. Non-obvious details: payload is JSON-serialized, schedule stores different value types depending on mode, handler is a registry key.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to each exported type with field-level documentation.

findings.title: Exported JobStore class has no JSDoc documentation
findings.description: JobStore is exported from the package entry point with 6 public methods and no JSDoc. Non-obvious behavior: constructor enables WAL mode, save() uses upsert semantics.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to JobStore class and its public methods.

findings.title: at(), every(), cron() methods share near-identical structure
findings.description: The three scheduling methods follow the exact same pattern (21 lines total, 7 lines each). The only variation is a one-line transformation of the first argument.
findings.suggestion: Extract the shared sequence into a private createJob() method, reducing the public methods to thin type-safe adapters.

findings.title: scheduleJob() silently ignores unregistered handler -- type gap
findings.description: Architecture finding tied to the type gap: the type system does not prevent scheduling jobs with unregistered handler names because `handler` is an unvalidated string.
findings.suggestion: Validate handler existence before saving, or use a generic parameter to constrain handler names at compile time.

findings.title: Switch in scheduleJob() could use a mode-to-config lookup
findings.description: The switch statement maps each mode to slightly different Cron constructor arguments. A small helper that produces {pattern, options} per mode would isolate mode-specific logic.
findings.suggestion: Extract mode dispatch into a private cronArgsForMode() method.

findings.title: save() uses 11 positional SQL parameters, hard to audit
findings.code_snippet:
this.db.prepare(`
  INSERT OR REPLACE INTO cron_jobs (...)
  VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
`).run(job.id, job.name, ...)
findings.description: 11 positional placeholders make column-to-value mapping error-prone. better-sqlite3 supports named parameters ($name) which are self-documenting.
findings.suggestion: Switch to named parameters ($id, $name, $mode, ...) with an object argument.

positive_observations: SQLite persistence with WAL mode and parameterized queries: All database operations use parameterized queries (? placeholders), eliminating SQL injection risk. WAL mode is correctly configured for concurrent read performance.
Real SQLite tests with temp files: Both test files use real better-sqlite3 with temporary directories, following the project's correct testing pattern. No InMemoryRepository or mock persistence.
Clean separation of concerns: Types (cron-types.ts), persistence (job-store.ts), and scheduling (cron-service.ts) are in separate files with clear responsibilities.
Proper cleanup in tests: afterEach hooks close the store/service and remove temp directories, preventing resource leaks between tests.
Database schema uses CHECK constraint for mode validation, providing defense-in-depth at the DB level.
Zero mock violations -- zero usage of vi.fn(), vi.mock(), InMemory*, Mock*, or Fake* patterns.
CronService integration tests use real timer-based assertions to verify jobs actually fire.
JobStore constructor uses try/catch with proper cleanup (db.close()) on init failure -- good resource management pattern.
Clean separation between CronService (scheduling logic) and JobStore (persistence).
ScheduleMode is a proper 3-value literal union type -- prevents invalid mode strings at compile time.
CronService class has good encapsulation: private store, private handlers Map, private activeJobs Map.
JobHandler type alias cleanly expresses async-compatible callback signature.
JobRow is a private internal interface correctly modeling the SQLite row shape separately from the domain type.
Zero forbidden comment patterns (no TODO, FIXME, HACK, PLACEHOLDER, or TEMP comments).
Type names and method signatures are reasonably self-documenting for internal use.
Both files are well under the 200-line guideline with clean, focused methods.
Guard clauses and early returns used consistently.
Pure mapRow function at module scope follows functional core pattern for data mapping.
JobStore test suite is thorough: CRUD operations, upsert behavior, persistence across reopens, all three schedule modes.
